summary: The Reedin Hyper Model combines Aluula construction with a high-performance freeride/big air design. The premium materials deliver exceptional weight savings for extended hangtime and broader wind range.
best_for: Premium big air/freeride riders wanting Aluula performance from Reedin

sources.summary: The Aluula construction brings noticeable improvements to the already-capable Reedin platform. Riders report impressive hangtime and low-end grunt.
Reviewer is impressed by the weight savings and how the Aluula construction translates into tangible performance gains in both light and strong wind.
Discussion focuses on whether Reedin's Aluula implementation competes with Ozone's and Duotone's premium offerings. Early consensus is very positive.

reviews.video_title: Big Air Kite Review 19 - Reedin HyperModel Brainchild

This review was brought to you thanks to more demo gear from the fine fellows at Mac Kiteboarding. So, as usu
reviews.verdict: ason of Montreal, to keep up to date with what I'm testing. I'm currently testing about six or seven other kites right now, but sadly I will be on holiday with my wife for the month of July. So, my apologies that there's likely to be a little bit of a gap before the next one, I'm afraid. But I will be uploading a lot of sessions and kite preview previews on my Insta in the meantime from what I've already been riding. So, thank you as always for making it this far. Until the next review, goodbye.
reviews.full_transcript: Hello, and welcome once again to another plus-sized kite review. In this review, we dive back into the co-design and manufacturing world of Brainchild with the Reeden Supermodel Brainchild Edition. This review was brought to you thanks to more demo gear from the fine fellows at Mac Kiteboarding. So, as usual, if you are interested in this kite, check out their website and hit up their YouTube channel for a whole lot more awesome kite content. I've put the link in the description. To quickly address a couple of the most common questions I also get from these reviews, I'll be recording my jumps with the Surfer app on my Apple Watch and filming using my GoPro 12 camera. If you do have any other questions about my reviews, this particular kite in general, um, or kites in general for that matter, kite surfing in New Zealand, or just want to help the channel grow, please hit me up by leaving a comment. And be sure to subscribe and share this review in any of your local kite surfing pages. With all of that housekeeping out of the way, let's talk about this awesome kite. So, right out of the bag, just like with the other Brainchild kites I've tested, the Super Model has that unique light, crispy feel to it. Um, unlike the other models from Harlem and F1, it does use a low V bar, which is nice if you're someone who's coming from obviously a low V quiver. You don't have to swap bars. However, I do personally find low V kites a little less responsive than Hi V now that I've ridden quite a lot of them back to back. Now, I couldn't of course tell you for sure how much of that is the line setup versus how much is the various kites that are using each setup, but to me there is a consistent slightly less direct feel when I swap back from high Y to the low V. Um, so that's something to keep in mind. Luckily, with this particular kite, like the other Brainchild kites, it is wildly light and very responsive. The kite feels really easy to pilot around the window and is very predictable as you ride around on it, but it doesn't feel like it has a lot of grunt, per se, um, compared to many of the top power hungry big air kites on the market or have the same tendency as other big air focused kites to want to shoot forward when you build up some power and line tension to send it. I wouldn't be surprised if this was intentional because the kite does feel like it has been specifically designed for progression at all levels with a very flexible free ride style. Good for a bit of everything with a more heavier leaning to things like wave and foil riding than many of the other big kites I tend to test. Thinking back to the kites I learned on, I would imagine this stall being very beneficial when you're just starting out as a beginner um and progressing jumper um as you won't get overpowered or uh pulled off the water too rapidly. Um but it is obviously a factor when you are a bit more advanced and wanting to jump as high as possible. In lighter to moderately powered winds, say under 30 knots, it's very hard to feel out of your depths on this kite, that's for sure. And despite its wild speed, it's lack of aggression does make it quite confidence boosting, at least uh until the wind starts to creep up, which I will get into a bit more as I break down the key areas. So, starting with boosting potential on the plus side, having a super fast kits means if you are particularly dialed in with it, you can get that sucker up to 12 with lightning fast speed. However, with that speed comes the added challenge of hitting the sweet spot for your boosts just right. And I think for the most part, I struggled with this despite riding the kite over several sessions while sharing other views. um I was doing when compared to say the trigger which I just reviewed, which I also was working on through the full time I was riding this, I rarely was able to hit the sweet spot as easily and consistently. So, I wasn't finding I was getting the same lift off of the water on the readon. Even when I wasn't over descending the kite and I did get my timing right, however, on the launch, the kite doesn't tend to gift you the same way some of the boostier kites do, like the NXT, Rebel, or even the Thrive or Trigger um from that Brainchild production. And to be able to get extra lift, you either need to get a little bit lucky or need to keep the kite very active through the air. In a similar way I mentioned with the Gong Kite I recently reviewed. I tend to have a pretty good idea of how high I'm going to jump when I'm using the surfer app. And if you've ever followed me on Instagram and seen my stories, sometimes when I'm doing commentary sessions, I'll do a little jump height guessing um through the session where I call out what I think is the height before the reading shows up. And most of the time I tend to be uh within a meter or so up to around 15 mters. But with this kite, even on my bigger jumps, on the windier sessions where I was getting the 10 to 12, 15 m, I was almost always overestimating my jumps by a couple of meters or so. And I think this was because I was getting the same kind of lift. I wasn't I just wasn't getting the same kind of lift, I should say, through the jump that I was expected based on the strength of wind and the other type of kites I'm more familiar with. Could this be from some on andoff wind? Definitely to some extent. Um, and this would also be from rider level as well. But when I'm reviewing kites, I am trying to figure out what potential each kite is for not only myself, but the majority of riders, particularly up to and around my level. I remember thinking while I was riding about this awesome video the surfer app team did of an insanely nuclear session in Cape Town at Macasar where a bunch of pros were going for the surfer record in like a 60 knot day and in the video although he was slightly behind some of the other top boosters Kevin was still jumping in the late 20s just shy of 30 m so you definitely can make the argument that with the right condition and skills you can can get right up there on this kite, but not many of us watching these reviews are among the best to ever do it. So, I certainly wouldn't list this kite among the top few boosters I've ridden and would rank it somewhere down around the level of perhaps the standard orbit or maybe even just below um if you can nail it just right. But I would say it's also got a smaller sweet spot than the orbit range. So that might make it tricky um for a lot of boosters getting new PBs. This level of power would also uh be similar in the hang time as well as although I was able to do some board offs um in this kite without needing too much height when my timing was on form. So certainly not bad, but the kite just doesn't have the same floaty feeling as the better big air orientated boosters that I'm used to. And to spend a little bit more time in the air, you really need to get the kite out wide and pile it around more above you uh to slowly create that added lift. The other kites I'm more familiar with and that I talk about a lot um tend to gift you this from just parking it above your head. At least the better ones, too. I think just straight up and down boosters who don't tend to hel loop and are just out for a cruise and woost could struggle a bit to get their timing right, both coming off the water and coming down from their jumps, as it's a little easy to overcend the kite, and the kite doesn't as easily want to catch you coming down. So, there might be a bit of teaagging for a lot of riders as they first adapt to its speed. Now, let's get on to looping. And as you would expect, this kite is one of the fastest loopers out there. And it is a lot of fun to loop with. Just like the Harlem Force or Thrive, it's lightning fast and loops super tight. So much so that it is quite hard to get the kite low or feel any power in your loops. This is of course great for the confidence uh for doing one-hand loops or for firthand or inexperienced loopers looking to advance in this area as the kite will always loop around super high and tight even in the smallest jumps. It also means this would be one of the better kites for looping in the larger sizes I would wager. But I was only able to test the 9 meter unlike uh this clip I have here of the upcoming peak video. The challenge for progressing, however, will be that because the kite is super fast and doesn't create a ton of lift, you might find yourself screwing up your heli loops at times if you don't already have those pretty dialed for smaller or faster kites. I'd bet that like me, a lot of riders will struggle a bit with sending it too quickly out of the window or even potentially pulling the heli loop a bit too tightly on the way down to the extent where you don't get a lot of lift out of it and might come in a little hotter than usual um on some that you would on some liftier kites rather. I didn't have too much trouble with it myself, I must say. Nor did any of my friends who are experienced loopers, especially just for standard loops. Um, because we're more used to faster kites by now. And I have done thousands of loops, particularly on my strong side. And the kite being very responsive and predictable um, helps as well. I certainly had to stay on my toes of course and keep it active to make sure I was getting softer landings usually with a wider heloop coming down particularly on my weaker looping side where I don't quite have the same feel I do going to the right. I also paid the price for my hubris once again with the brainchild kite on this one, just like I did with the trigger where I thought after a few sessions I was finally getting totally dialed with it and the fact I was in a seat harness that I'm still wearing because of my ribs. So, I went for this boogie just as the wind was kicking in and I think I had a nice gust through the loop which took me quite a bit further into the beach than I was expecting. even um giving me so much rotation I had to add a second uh one on the way down. Um and that was the biggest loop I actually had of the whole session. Um and as you can see, I landed in very little water and had a little bit of a roly pololy as I accidentally also then looped my lines around the bar. Now, this is not the kite's fault, but seeing I recorded the clip and I took the hit, why not share it in the review to show you the commitment you're getting from this content. So, having seen that, I think it's fair to say that once you are used to its flight characteristics, you will get a fair bit of confidence on this kite. Um, and how you use it may be up to you and your own stupidity. like looping. Confidence boosting is one of the stronger points of this kite because the combination of predictability, lighter bar pressure, and less aggressive power make for a very friendly ride. But as the wind does creep up over 30 knots, at least on the 9 m um and other smaller sizes, however, I think it would get quite challenging for a lot of riders just because of the kite's speed, particularly with nailing your timing, both jumping off the water and coming down as cleanly as possible. Uh from sizes, say 10 and up, I think anybody can ride this very happily. The kite is also a three strut model, so as the wind creeps up, it does lose some of its shape at times in the gus and become more challenging to control. Well, um I found that it got more challenging than the Thrive, which did lose its shape as well, but seemed to cut through that uh despite despite it flopping around. Certainly a more experienced rider of course would like that insane speed and directional performance, but I wouldn't say it would be an ideal kite specifically for a big air focused rider only due to its lack of power and hang time, but works very well for progressing not only big air riders, but just general free riders. For me personally, being that my main focus is big air and that is also the main focus of these reviews, I think some of the best features of this kite would be a little lost on me as it is a more multid-disiplined uh focused kite and really seems to balance nicely with foil and wave riding as well. So, if you're into those disciplines, you'll probably love this kite. Um, when I was thinking about the overall summary of this kite, I was realizing that after doing these reviews for a few years, and considering that I have certainly improved a fair bit as a rider, at least for someone over 40, that is, although I try to be as consistent as possible from review to review, I do think that my own preferences have evolved a little bit with the kites. If you go back to my first review, because I had been riding standard orbits for a few years at that stage, I was still really into just very loop friendly lighter bar pressure direct kites just like this region. So, if I had have reviewed this at the time, it would have probably been the top of the heap because because of how fast it was back then. Although I loved getting out my XR7 and sending big single loops and woosting when the wind was strong, my preferred style of riding was always about loops and loop progression um rather than just power and hang time. So that didn't play as big a factor for me um because even at the time I'd never even done a board off. Nowadays, I tend to lean a little bit more to kites that still loop well, but are also easier to jump high. Um, have a bigger sweet spot and more hang time. Um, so I can combine a few different types of tricks. Don't get me wrong, there wasn't a session where I didn't have an absolute blast on this kite, but I did get disappointed quite a few times where on jumps where I felt I should have gotten a wee bit higher. Um, but comparing my surfer scores to my usual friends on those sessions, they were all just a slight drop off. I think if we had more consistent uh surf or wave riding here in Auckland, I would also love to have uh one of these in my quiver for a day where I would get to do some strapless and then also want to send some loops afterwards. While I was on this kite, I was actually thinking back to um my time in Maitius a few years ago, learning to ride a surfboard on my orbit, and this kite would have just been amazing for that wind in those conditions in that sort of mid20s range all the time where you can do a little bit of everything. There is an old Maitius edit I actually made kiting around Leourne on my YouTube if you want to check that out, too. So, if you are purely interested in big air, perhaps at least in the smaller sizes, this isn't the best option out there. But if you are looking to have just a lot of fun, um progress with some confidence and specifically do multiple disciplines, then this is certainly one of the best kites on the market for that. Uh, so if you have tried this kite and have any additional feedback you'd like to add in or anything from my recent reviews, have some thoughts, please throw them in the comments. Or if you think I'm way off, throw that in there, too, because comments really help these videos grow and allows me to do more of them. Don't forget to like and subscribe and follow me on Instagram, Jason of Montreal, to keep up to date with what I'm testing. I'm currently testing about six or seven other kites right now, but sadly I will be on holiday with my wife for the month of July. So, my apologies that there's likely to be a little bit of a gap before the next one, I'm afraid. But I will be uploading a lot of sessions and kite preview previews on my Insta in the meantime from what I've already been riding. So, thank you as always for making it this far. Until the next review, goodbye.
